mission.title: Install Red Hat AI Inference Server on OpenShift
mission.description: Red Hat AI Inference Server (RHAIIS) is Red Hat's productized build of the vLLM inference server, distributed as a hardened container image and installed on OpenShift through the OpenShift AI operator (RHOAI) or as a standalone Deployment on a GPU-enabled node. It exposes an OpenAI-compatible HTTP API at `/v1/chat/completions`, supports paged attention and tensor parallelism from upstream vLLM, and adds Red Hat test coverage and support entitlements for supported models. This mission walks through the standalone Deployment path using the `registry.redhat.io/rhaiis/vllm-cuda-rhel9` image on a node with an NVIDIA GPU and the NVIDIA GPU Operator already installed. Upstream docs: https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_ai_inference_server/ .

steps.title: Confirm OpenShift prerequisites
steps.description:
RHAIIS is supported on OpenShift 4.15+ with at least one node exposing `nvidia.com/gpu` through the NVIDIA GPU Operator. You also need pull access to `registry.redhat.io` — typically via a RH subscription pull secret already wired into the cluster at install time. Verify:
```bash
oc version
oc get nodes -o custom-columns=NAME:.metadata.name,GPU:.status.capacity.nvidia\.com/gpu
oc get clusterserviceversion -n nvidia-gpu-operator | grep gpu-operator
```
If the GPU operator is not installed, follow the Red Hat docs at https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/openshift/ to install it before continuing.

steps.title: Create the project and service account
steps.description:
Create a dedicated OpenShift project for RHAIIS and a service account for the workload. The pod needs the `nvidia-driver-validator` SELinux context to access the GPU device plugin, which the GPU operator provides via its own SCC:
```bash
oc new-project rhaiis
oc create serviceaccount rhaiis-runner -n rhaiis
```

steps.title: Create a pull secret for registry.redhat.io
steps.description:
RHAIIS images live in `registry.redhat.io`, which requires an authenticated pull. If your cluster's global pull secret already includes `registry.redhat.io`, you can skip this step. Otherwise, create a namespace-scoped secret from your Red Hat service account token (generate one at https://access.redhat.com/terms-based-registry ):
```bash
oc create secret docker-registry rhaiis-pull \
  --docker-server=registry.redhat.io \
  --docker-username='<service-account-name>' \
  --docker-password='<service-account-token>' \
  -n rhaiis
oc secrets link rhaiis-runner rhaiis-pull --for=pull -n rhaiis
```
Never commit the secret YAML to source control.

steps.title: Create a PVC for the model cache
steps.description:
vLLM loads safetensors from a local path. For anything larger than a few GB, back the path with a PVC so the model survives pod restarts:
```bash
cat <<'EOF' | oc apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: rhaiis-models
  namespace: rhaiis
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 60Gi
EOF
```
Raise the size if your target model is >30 B parameters.

steps.title: Deploy RHAIIS
steps.description:
This Deployment runs the Red Hat vLLM image, reserves one GPU, mounts the PVC at `/opt/app-root/src/.cache/huggingface`, and starts the server with a representative chat-instruct model. Replace `MODEL_ID` with the HF model identifier you want to serve — the example uses Mistral 7B Instruct v0.3, which fits on a single 24 GB GPU:
```bash
cat <<'EOF' | oc apply -f -
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: rhaiis
  namespace: rhaiis
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: rhaiis
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: rhaiis
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: rhaiis-runner
      containers:
        - name: vllm
          image: registry.redhat.io/rhaiis/vllm-cuda-rhel9:latest
          args:
            - --model
            - mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3
            - --host
            - 0.0.0.0
            - --port
            - "8000"
          env:
            - name: HF_HOME
              value: /opt/app-root/src/.cache/huggingface
          ports:
            - containerPort: 8000
              name: http
          resources:
            requests:
              cpu: "4"
              memory: 16Gi
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
            limits:
              cpu: "8"
              memory: 32Gi
              nvidia.com/gpu: "1"
          volumeMounts:
            - name: models
              mountPath: /opt/app-root/src/.cache/huggingface
          readinessProbe:
            httpGet:
              path: /health
              port: 8000
            initialDelaySeconds: 120
            periodSeconds: 10
      volumes:
        - name: models
          persistentVolumeClaim:
            claimName: rhaiis-models
EOF
oc -n rhaiis rollout status deploy/rhaiis --timeout=15m
```
The first start takes a while because the container fetches the model from Hugging Face into the PVC.

steps.title: Expose RHAIIS as a Service
steps.description:
Create a ClusterIP Service so clients inside the cluster reach the endpoint at `http://rhaiis.rhaiis.svc.cluster.local:8000`:
```bash
cat <<'EOF' | oc apply -f -
apiVersion: v1
kind: Service
metadata:
  name: rhaiis
  namespace: rhaiis
spec:
  type: ClusterIP
  selector:
    app: rhaiis
  ports:
    - name: http
      port: 8000
      targetPort: 8000
EOF
```

steps.title: Verify the OpenAI-compatible endpoint
steps.description:
Port-forward the Service and confirm inference responds:
```bash
oc -n rhaiis port-forward svc/rhaiis 8000:8000
```
In a second terminal:
```bash
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/models
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/chat/completions -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"model":"mistralai/Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3","messages":[{"role":"user","content":"Say hi in one word."}]}'
```

resolution.summary: A successful RHAIIS install has the `rhaiis` Deployment in the `rhaiis` project reporting one Ready replica, the GPU allocation visible on `oc describe node`, `/v1/models` returning the loaded model identifier, and `/v1/chat/completions` returning an OpenAI-compatible response with the expected token usage.

uninstall.title: Delete the Deployment and Service
uninstall.description:
Remove the workload first so the GPU is released:
```bash
oc -n rhaiis delete deploy/rhaiis svc/rhaiis --ignore-not-found
```

uninstall.title: Delete the model PVC
uninstall.description:
Only delete the PVC if you want to reclaim the model cache storage. It can be large (tens of GB for a 7B-class model):
```bash
oc -n rhaiis delete pvc/rhaiis-models --ignore-not-found
```

uninstall.title: Delete the project
uninstall.description:
Remove the namespace-scoped service account, pull secret, and project:
```bash
oc delete project rhaiis --ignore-not-found
```

upgrade.title: Bump the image tag
upgrade.description:
Red Hat publishes new RHAIIS tags alongside each upstream vLLM release plus backport builds. Check https://catalog.redhat.com/software/containers/rhaiis/vllm-cuda-rhel9 for the current supported tag and roll it out:
```bash
oc -n rhaiis set image deploy/rhaiis vllm=registry.redhat.io/rhaiis/vllm-cuda-rhel9:<new-tag>
oc -n rhaiis rollout status deploy/rhaiis --timeout=15m
```

upgrade.title: Swap the served model
upgrade.description:
To switch models, edit the `--model` arg on the Deployment. The runtime will drop the currently loaded model and load the new one from the PVC cache (or re-download from Hugging Face if it is not yet cached). Restart the Deployment after the edit:
```bash
oc -n rhaiis set env deploy/rhaiis MODEL_ID=<new-model-id>
oc -n rhaiis rollout restart deploy/rhaiis
```

troubleshooting.title: ImagePullBackOff from registry.redhat.io
troubleshooting.description:
Pull failures against `registry.redhat.io` are almost always authentication issues. Confirm your pull secret is linked to the service account and check the pod events:
```bash
oc -n rhaiis describe pod -l app=rhaiis
oc -n rhaiis get sa rhaiis-runner -o jsonpath='{.imagePullSecrets}'
```
If the global cluster pull secret is the expected path, update it with `oc set data secret/pull-secret -n openshift-config --from-file=.dockerconfigjson=<path>` and restart the kubelet on the target node.

troubleshooting.title: Pod stuck with insufficient GPU
troubleshooting.description:
RHAIIS requires at least one `nvidia.com/gpu` on the target node. If the pod is Pending with an insufficient resources event, verify the GPU operator health:
```bash
oc get nodes -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.allocatable.nvidia\.com/gpu}{"\n"}{end}'
oc get pods -n nvidia-gpu-operator
```
If GPU-operator pods are themselves unhealthy, follow https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/openshift/ for the operator troubleshooting guide.

troubleshooting.title: OOM on a large model
troubleshooting.description: A 7B-class model in fp16 uses ~14 GB of VRAM; larger models need quantization or tensor parallelism across multiple GPUs. If the container is being OOMKilled by the GPU, either switch to a smaller model or pass `--tensor-parallel-size <N>` to spread the model across N GPUs on the same node (and raise `nvidia.com/gpu` requests accordingly).

troubleshooting.title: Readiness probe timing out
troubleshooting.description: The first start-up takes a long time because vLLM downloads model weights from Hugging Face into the PVC. If `rollout status` times out, check the container logs for the `Loading model` progress line and raise the readiness probe's `initialDelaySeconds`. Once the PVC is warm, subsequent restarts complete in under a minute.

security.title: OpenShift SCCs and the GPU operator
security.description: Running RHAIIS on OpenShift requires the NVIDIA GPU operator to be installed first. The GPU operator itself brings in several SecurityContextConstraints that grant the driver pods host-level access. This is a prerequisite for any CUDA workload — review the operator's SCC footprint at https://docs.nvidia.com/datacenter/cloud-native/openshift/ before approving it. The RHAIIS container itself does NOT need hostNetwork, hostPID, or hostPath mounts, and runs under the `restricted-v2` SCC once the GPU plumbing is in place.

security.title: registry.redhat.io authentication
security.description: RHAIIS images are pulled from `registry.redhat.io`, which requires a Red Hat authenticated pull. Use a subscription-scoped service account token — never share a user's personal token. Store the pull secret in a Kubernetes Secret and link it to the RHAIIS service account. For cluster-wide access, the OpenShift installer's global pull secret is the right place; for namespace-scoped access, link the Secret to the service account via `oc secrets link`.

security.title: Red Hat supportability and CVE coverage
security.description: The point of using RHAIIS over upstream vLLM is that Red Hat provides CVE backports, FIPS-validated image builds, and a supported base layer on top of the same vLLM engine. Keep images up to date by subscribing to Red Hat product security advisories at https://access.redhat.com/security/updates/advisory/ and tracking the RHAIIS release notes at https://docs.redhat.com/en/documentation/red_hat_ai_inference_server/ .

security.title: Model provenance
security.description: RHAIIS loads Hugging Face safetensors like upstream vLLM. Red Hat's supportability extends to the runtime, not to arbitrary third-party models — using a model that is not on the RH supported-models list is allowed, but outside of RH support. For production, prefer models with a clear provenance, a permissive license, and a published safety evaluation.

security.title: Relationship to the KubeStellar Console SECURITY-MODEL
security.description: The Console's AI-agent code path is architected so user chat content and command output reach the LLM provider, but kubeconfig files and cluster credentials never do — see docs/security/SECURITY-MODEL.md in the kubestellar/console repo. RHAIIS is the strongest enterprise posture the Console can point at: inference runs inside the OpenShift cluster, the image ships from a Red Hat registry, and there is no egress to an external model provider at steady state.

prerequisites.description: OpenShift 4.15+ with the NVIDIA GPU Operator installed and at least one node exposing `nvidia.com/gpu`. An authenticated pull against `registry.redhat.io` (subscription-scoped service account token) and a default StorageClass supporting ReadWriteOnce volumes. Outbound egress to huggingface.co is required on first start for the model fetch.
